1.lecture:
§1 · The six-family taxonomy

Processing operations transform a workpart. Shaping covers four distinct routes: casting and molding (start liquid), deformation — rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing, sheet forming (start solid, move it), particulate processing (press and sinter powders), and material removal (machining, grinding, cutting away). Property-enhancing operations — heat treatment above all — leave geometry unchanged and rebuild microstructure instead. Surface processing — cleaning, coating, plating — changes only the surface. Assembly operations join parts: permanent joining (welding, brazing, adhesives) and mechanical assembly (bolting, riveting, press fits). A seventh box, material handling and inspection, adds no value directly but consumes most of the lead time in a real plant.

§2 · Primary vs. secondary, and reading a real process chain

Primary processing creates the basic shape — a billet, a casting — from raw material. Secondary processing refines that shape further — machining a bearing seat, grinding a journal onto an already-cast or already-forged part. The working method: trace any product from raw material to dispatch, naming which family each step belongs to. A rebar's chain is casting (shaping, primary) then hot rolling (shaping, deformation) then cooling, cutting, and bundling (handling) — two value-adding families for one product. A power-cable conductor's chain runs longer: wire drawing (deformation), annealing (property-enhancing), stranding (mechanical assembly), insulation extrusion over the conductor (shaping, polymer), spooling and test (handling, inspection) — four families in a single line.

§3 · Why this taxonomy matters more than it looks

By tonnage, the world's metal is shaped overwhelmingly by casting and deformation, not machining — machining refines a surface or a fit onto a shape one of those other families already created. An engineer walking into "manufacturing" expecting mainly a machine shop is statistically far more likely to meet a caster, a rolling mill, a press, or a filling line as the dominant floor space. Casting and deformation are not legacy technology on the way out; they remain the primary-shaping backbone of everything heavier than electronics. What has changed around them is instrumentation, automation, and control — exactly where this curriculum's later Industry 4.0 material points.

§4 · Working method — reading any plant with one map

Given an unfamiliar plant or process line: (1) identify the raw material entering; (2) trace each transformation step and classify it into one of the six families; (3) note where primary shaping ends and secondary refinement begins; (4) recognize that handling and inspection, though value-neutral, often dominate real lead time and hide a large share of any real improvement opportunity. This single procedure reads a steel mill, a cable plant, or a food-filling hall with equal clarity.

Key fact

By tonnage, casting and deformation shape the large majority of the world's metal — machining refines a shape another process already created, it does not usually create the shape itself.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • No prior manufacturing coursework — this lesson is the map the rest of the course hangs on.
  • General familiarity with the idea of a factory or production line, at a common-sense level.

Toolkit — the six families

FamilyWhat it doesExample
Shaping — casting/moldingStarts liquid, freezes to shapeSand casting, die casting
Shaping — deformationStarts solid, moves materialRolling, forging, extrusion, drawing
Shaping — particulate/removalPress+sinter powder / cuts material awayPowder metallurgy; machining
Property-enhancingGeometry unchanged, microstructure rebuiltHeat treatment
Surface processingChanges the surface onlyCoating, plating, cleaning
Assembly (permanent / mechanical)Joins partsWelding; bolting, riveting

quiz.q:
Classifying a bracket production chain

Problem. A steel bracket is produced: (a) cut from a continuously cast billet, (b) hot-forged to rough shape, (c) heat-treated, (d) machined for mounting holes, (e) painted. Name the family for each step.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) shaping/casting, primary. (b) shaping/deformation. (c) property-enhancing. (d) shaping/material removal, secondary. (e) surface processing.

quiz.q:
Classifying a conductor process chain

Problem. A cable conductor’s chain is: wire drawing → annealing → stranding → polymer extrusion over the conductor → spooling. Identify which steps are primary shaping, which are property-modifying, and which are assembly.
quiz.solution: Solution. Wire drawing: primary shaping (deformation). Annealing: property-enhancing, not shaping at all — it restores ductility lost to the drawing passes. Stranding: mechanical assembly (twisting wires together). Polymer extrusion: shaping (a second, different material, primary for the insulation layer). Spooling: handling.

quiz.q:
Why "machining" is the wrong first guess

Problem. Using tonnage reasoning, explain why a new mechanical engineer expecting to mainly encounter machining on a first plant tour is likely mistaken, and name the two families more likely to dominate by volume.
quiz.solution: Solution. By tonnage, casting and deformation dominate world metal production; machining is a secondary process applied after a primary shape from one of those two families already exists. A plant tour is statistically far more likely to feature a caster, a rolling mill, or a press occupying the dominant floor space than a machine shop.

quiz.q: Casting and molding are classified as:
choices: A shaping operation that starts liquid
quiz.solution: Casting starts from a liquid and freezes to the cavity's shape.

quiz.q: Deformation processes (rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing) are classified as:
choices: Shaping operations that start solid and move material
quiz.solution: These all move already-solid material into a new shape.

quiz.q: Heat treatment is classified as:
choices: A property-enhancing operation — geometry unchanged, microstructure rebuilt
quiz.solution: Heat treatment changes internal structure, not external geometry.

quiz.q: Welding and brazing are classified as:
quiz.solution: They join separate parts into one, permanently.

quiz.q: Material handling and inspection:
choices: Add no value directly but often dominate real lead time
Are the highest value-adding operations in a plant
Never appear in a real process chain
quiz.solution: Necessary, but value-neutral — and usually where most elapsed time actually goes.

quiz.q: Primary processing:
choices: Creates the basic shape from raw material
Refines a shape another process already created
quiz.solution: Primary shaping is the first shape-creating step from raw material.

quiz.q: Secondary processing:
choices: Refines a shape a primary process already created
quiz.solution: Machining a bearing seat onto an already-cast part is the classic example.

quiz.q: By tonnage, the world's metal is shaped mostly by:
quiz.solution: Machining refines a shape; it rarely creates the bulk shape itself.

1.kuwait:
A rebar's process chain in a regional steel mill runs scrap through melting, then continuous casting (shaping: casting, primary), then hot rolling (shaping: deformation), then cooling, cutting, and bundling (handling) — two value-adding families producing one product. A power-cable conductor at a regional cable plant runs a longer chain: rod drawing (deformation), annealing (property-enhancing), stranding (mechanical assembly), insulation extrusion over the conductor (shaping: polymer), spooling and test (handling, inspection) — four families in one line, which is why cable-plant engineers tend to be generalists rather than specialists in a single family.

Reading an unfamiliar plant with this six-family map, rather than assuming every operation is either "the machine shop" or "the assembly line," is often the fastest way a new engineer orients on an unfamiliar floor — a dairy filling hall, a fabrication yard, and a steel mill all decompose into the same six boxes, just in very different proportions.

2.lecture:
§1 · Casting is a controlled thermal collapse

A casting process melts metal, pours it into a cavity shaped like the part, and lets it solidify — the oldest manufacturing process still run at industrial scale, surviving because nothing else produces complex internal geometry in one operation this cheaply. Expendable-mold processes (sand, shell, investment) destroy the mold to retrieve the part — cheap tooling, high geometric freedom. Permanent-mold processes (die casting, continuous casting) reuse a metal mold — expensive tooling that demands volume to pay for itself. This lecture develops the physics common to all of them using sand casting, since its assumptions are cleanest: pay energy to superheat the melt, spend that superheat transporting metal through the gating system before it freezes in the channels, then extract all of it back out through the mold wall at a rate the mold controls — and the volume deficit left behind by that extraction is the riser's problem.

§2 · Gating: Bernoulli with consequences

The gating system has one job: deliver the melt to the cavity before superheat runs out, without turbulence tearing air and oxide into the stream. Applying Bernoulli's equation from the free metal surface in the pouring basin to the sprue base — both stations near atmospheric pressure, the basin kept topped up so its surface barely moves — gives Torricelli's result, \( v=\sqrt{2gh} \), with \(h\) the vertical head from the free metal surface, never from the sprue's own top. Because velocity rises with depth, a parallel-walled sprue lets the falling stream neck away from its walls and aspirate air; matching the sprue's taper to the falling jet via continuity, \( A_t/A_b=\sqrt{h_b/h_t} \), removes that defect with one line of algebra. The flow is, in practice, always fully turbulent (Reynolds numbers in the tens of thousands for liquid metal) — the designer's real tools are geometry (taper, gate area, filters), not pouring speed.

§3 · Solidification, and the equation that runs the foundry

A pure metal freezes at one temperature; an alloy freezes across a range, liquidus to solidus, with a mushy zone of dendritic growth between them. For a sand mold — a poor enough conductor that the mold, not the metal, controls the rate — treating the mold wall as a semi-infinite solid gives a clean result: total solidification time follows Chvorinov's rule, \[ TST=C_m\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n},\qquad n\approx2\text{ for sand molds}, \] where \(V/A\) is the casting's modulus. \(C_m\) is measured once from a test casting in the same sand and alloy, then the rule ranks every shape: thick, chunky regions (high modulus) freeze last; thin fins (low modulus) freeze first. This mold-controlled assumption fails in a water-cooled metal die, where interface and metal-side resistance dominate instead and \(n\) drifts toward 1 — carrying \(n=2\) into a die-casting estimate is a real, not cosmetic, error.

§4 · Shrinkage, and the riser that pays for it

Metal contracts three separate times, compensated by three different people. Liquid contraction (pouring temperature down to freezing) is cheap — the gating system simply supplies a little more liquid while everything is still fluid. Solidification shrinkage — the liquid-to-solid density jump, roughly \(6\)–\(7\)% for aluminum, \(2.5\)–\(3\)% for low-carbon steel — is the riser's job: a sacrificial reservoir that must still be liquid, and must stay liquid longer than everything it feeds, or the shrinkage cavity forms in the part instead. Chvorinov turns "stay liquid longer" into arithmetic: sizing the riser so \( TST_{riser}=1.25\times TST_{casting} \) — a 25% margin covering what the model ignores — translates directly into a required riser modulus, \( (V/A)_{riser}=\sqrt{1.25}\,(V/A)_{casting} \). Solid contraction (freezing point down to room temperature) has no liquid left to feed it at all; the patternmaker builds the pattern oversize by a shrinkage allowance instead, so the part contracts down onto the drawing.

Key rule — Chvorinov (1940)

\[ TST = C_m\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n},\quad n\approx2\ (\text{sand}).\qquad (V/A)_{riser}=\sqrt{1.25}\,(V/A)_{casting}. \]
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Sensible vs. latent heat (Thermodynamics I) — the pouring energy balance.
  • Bernoulli's equation and continuity (Fluid Mechanics) — the gating derivation.
  • Transient conduction into a semi-infinite solid (Heat Transfer) — the basis of Chvorinov's rule.

Toolkit — gating and solidification

TermFormulaUse
Sprue-base velocity\( v=\sqrt{2gh} \)Torricelli, from the free-surface head.
Anti-aspiration taper\( A_t/A_b=\sqrt{h_b/h_t} \)Keeps the stream sealed to the sprue wall.
Chvorinov's rule\( TST=C_m(V/A)^2 \)Ranks shapes by modulus, not volume.
Riser sizing rule\( (V/A)_r=\sqrt{1.25}\,(V/A)_c \)Riser outlives the casting by 25% TST.
Solidification shrinkage~6-7% (Al), ~2.5-3% (steel)What the riser feeds.

quiz.q:
Sprue base velocity and taper

Problem. A pouring basin holds 50 mm of metal above a 200 mm sprue. Find the velocity at the sprue base, and the top-to-base area ratio needed to prevent aspiration.
quiz.solution: Solution. Head at the base: \(h_b=0.050+0.200=0.250\ \text{m}\). \( v=\sqrt{2\times9.81\times0.250}\approx2.21\ \text{m/s} \). Head at the top is the basin depth alone, \(h_t=0.050\ \text{m}\): \( A_t/A_b=\sqrt{h_b/h_t}=\sqrt{0.250/0.050}=\sqrt5\approx2.24 \) — the sprue narrows by that factor from top to base.

quiz.q:
Sizing a riser by Chvorinov’s rule

Problem. A steel plate casting has modulus \((V/A)_c=2.31\ \text{cm}\), and the mold constant is \(C_m=2.0\ \text{min/cm}^2\). Find the casting’s solidification time and the modulus a riser needs to satisfy the 25% rule.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( TST_c=2.0\times2.31^2\approx10.7\ \text{min} \). Required \( TST_r=1.25\times10.7\approx13.3\ \text{min} \), so \( (V/A)_r=\sqrt{13.3/2.0}\approx2.58\ \text{cm} \) — larger than the casting’s modulus, confirming the riser freezes last.

quiz.q:
Cube vs. sphere — same volume, different modulus

Problem. A cube and a sphere of the same alloy, same volume 512 cm\(^3\) (the cube is 8 cm on a side), are poured in the same sand, \(C_m=1.8\ \text{min/cm}^2\). Which solidifies last, and by roughly how much?
quiz.solution: Solution. Cube: \(A=6\times8^2=384\ \text{cm}^2\), \(V/A=1.33\ \text{cm}\), \(TST=1.8\times1.33^2\approx3.2\ \text{min}\). Sphere: \(r\approx4.96\ \text{cm}\), \(A=4\pi r^2\approx309\ \text{cm}^2\), \(V/A=r/3\approx1.65\ \text{cm}\), \(TST\approx1.8\times1.65^2\approx4.9\ \text{min}\). The sphere solidifies last, by roughly 1.7 minutes — over 50% longer — despite identical volume, because Chvorinov ranks by surface area exposed, not volume.

quiz.q: The velocity at the base of a sprue is given by:
choices: v = √(2gh), with h measured from the free metal surface
v = √(2gh), with h the sprue's own length
v is independent of head height
quiz.solution: The datum is the free metal surface in the basin, not the sprue top — a classic source of error.

quiz.q: A tapered sprue is used mainly to:
choices: Prevent the falling stream from pulling away from the walls and aspirating air
quiz.solution: Matching the taper to the accelerating jet keeps the stream sealed against the sprue wall.

quiz.q: Chvorinov's rule ranks casting sections by:
quiz.solution: A shape hiding more volume behind less surface freezes later, regardless of total size.

quiz.q: Chvorinov's exponent n≈2 specifically assumes:
choices: Mold-controlled heat flow, as in an insulating sand mold
quiz.solution: In a metal die, interface/metal-side resistance dominates instead and n drifts toward 1.

quiz.q: Liquid contraction, the first of the three contractions, is compensated by:
choices: The gating system supplying a little extra liquid while still fluid
quiz.solution: Cheap and automatic, as long as the gating system stays connected and fluid.

quiz.q: Solidification shrinkage — the liquid-to-solid density jump — is compensated by:
quiz.solution: This is specifically what a riser exists to feed.

quiz.q: Solid contraction, after the part is fully frozen, is compensated by:
choices: The patternmaker building the pattern oversize (shrinkage allowance)
It cannot be compensated at all
quiz.solution: No liquid remains to feed this contraction — only an oversized pattern corrects for it.

quiz.q: A riser sized by the 25% rule has a required modulus:
choices: Larger than the casting's own modulus, by a factor of √1.25
Smaller than the casting's own modulus
Exactly equal to the casting's modulus
quiz.solution: A larger modulus means a longer solidification time — the riser must outlast the casting.

2.kuwait:
A foundry engineer designing a gating system runs §2 exactly as derived: head measured from the free metal surface, sprue taper set by the area-ratio rule, and gate sizing chosen to slow the stream below the velocity that entrains oxide — get the datum for h wrong, and every downstream number (velocity, fill time, taper) inherits the error.

The same Chvorinov analysis that sizes a riser also scales up directly to continuous casting — a solid shell growing inward from a water-cooled mold at steady state, governed by the identical square-root-of-time physics. A regional steel mill running a billet caster around the clock is this lesson's physics in continuous operation rather than a single pour; that machine gets its own full treatment next lesson.

3.lecture:
§1 · Patterns, allowances, and molding sand

A mold cavity is deliberately wrong, in four specific ways. The shrinkage (patternmaker's) allowance builds the pattern oversize so solid contraction shrinks the casting back onto the drawing — it cannot compensate solidification shrinkage, which is the riser's job (Lesson 2). Draft, a taper of roughly \(1\)–\(3^\circ\) on surfaces perpendicular to the parting line, lets the pattern withdraw without tearing the sand. Machining allowance leaves extra stock on surfaces to be machined. Distortion allowance pre-bends a pattern opposite to its predicted distortion. Internal geometry comes from cores — bonded-sand shapes that are also a gas source and a restraint on contraction. Green sand itself is specified by test numbers, not by feel: green compressive strength (too low risks collapse; too high traps gas and resists contraction), permeability (how well gases vent — low permeability plus wet sand is a blowhole problem waiting to happen), and moisture (too dry crumbles, too wet boils).

§2 · Fluidity, misruns, and cold shuts

Fluidity, in the foundry sense, is measured, not derived — the distance a standard test channel fills before the stream freezes shut. It is a race between the advancing stream and mold-controlled heat loss, and it rises with superheat, falls with a wide freezing range (a mushy alloy clogs the channel before it is fully solid), and falls with a stiff surface oxide film. When the race is lost, the resulting defect has two names with two different causes: a misrun is a section the metal never reached at all; a cold shut is where two streams met but were each too cold to fuse, leaving an oxide-lined seam that can look deceptively like a crack. The fixes differ — a misrun asks for more superheat or faster filling; a cold shut additionally asks for gating that avoids opposed, long-path streams.

§3 · Gas porosity, and Sievert's law derived

Liquid metal dissolves gas far more readily than solid metal does — that solubility cliff at the freezing front is the entire mechanism of gas porosity. Hydrogen dissolves by splitting into two atoms, \( \text{H}_2\rightleftharpoons2[\text{H}] \), so the equilibrium constant carries the dissolved concentration squared against the gas partial pressure: \( K(T)=[\text{H}]^2/p_{\text{H}_2} \). Solving gives Sievert's law, \[ [\text{H}]=S_0(T)\sqrt{p/p_0}, \] a square root that is not an empirical fit — it is the stoichiometry of the dissolution reaction doing algebra. \(S_0\) rises steeply with temperature, the hidden price of superheat: a hotter melt runs farther (§02) and dissolves more hydrogen from every damp tool or humid hour. At solidification, solubility drops sharply, the rejected gas recombines, and the result is pinholes — small, spherical, smooth-walled pores, their roundness the signature of having grown as bubbles in liquid. Mold and core gas — steam and binder combustion products that could not vent through the sand — is the other source, producing larger, often subsurface blowholes instead.

§4 · Shrinkage porosity, and the working method for a defect

Solidification shrinkage the riser fails to feed becomes a cavity, at two scales. Macro-shrinkage is an open pipe or closed cavity at an unfed hot spot — a feeding-path failure, visible once sectioned. Microporosity is subtler: in the mushy zone of a wide-freezing-range alloy, the last interdendritic liquid is cut off by the surrounding dendrite forest and shrinks in place, leaving jagged, angular, tree-lined voids — a morphology that contrasts directly with gas porosity's round, smooth pores. The working method for any defect: (1) read the morphology — round and smooth points to gas, jagged and angular points to shrinkage; (2) trace it to its physical cause using the taxonomy — solubility drop, volume deficit, lost heat race, restrained contraction, mold failure, or melt cleanliness; (3) let the cause pick the fix, not the appearance alone, since a misrun and a cold shut can look similar but need different corrections.

Key tool — Sievert's law

\[ [\text{H}] = S_0(T)\sqrt{p/p_0} \]

A square-root relation from reaction stoichiometry — the mechanism behind pinhole porosity.
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Chvorinov's rule and the three contractions (Lesson 2).
  • Chemical equilibrium — the equilibrium constant K(T) — from first-year chemistry.

Toolkit — casting defects

FamilyCauseSignature
Gas porosity (pinholes)Solubility drop on freezingSmall, round, smooth-walled
Mold/core gas (blowholes)Steam/binder gas can't ventLarger, subsurface
Macro-shrinkageUnfed volume deficitOpen pipe or closed cavity
MicroporosityInterdendritic liquid cut offJagged, angular, tree-lined
Misrun / cold shutLost the fluidity raceMissing section / cold-fused seam

quiz.q:
Reading a defect’s morphology

Problem. A sectioned casting shows small, round, smooth-walled pores scattered near the upper surface. A second casting shows jagged, angular voids threaded between visible dendrite arms near its thermal center. Identify each defect family and its physical cause.
quiz.solution: Solution. The first is gas porosity (pinholes) — round, smooth pores are the signature of gas bubbles that grew in the liquid before being trapped, caused by a solubility drop at freezing (Sievert's law). The second is microporosity — angular, interdendritic voids caused by the last liquid being cut off from feed metal in the mushy zone, a volume-deficit (shrinkage) cause, not a gas cause.

quiz.q:
Applying Sievert’s law

Problem. At a reference pressure \(p_0\), a melt dissolves \(S_0=25\) ppm of hydrogen. If the effective hydrogen partial pressure is raised to \(4p_0\) (from humid tooling), find the new dissolved concentration.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( [\text{H}]=S_0\sqrt{p/p_0}=25\times\sqrt4=25\times2=50\) ppm — doubling, not quadrupling, because the relationship is a square root of pressure, direct from the \(\text{H}_2\rightleftharpoons2[\text{H}]\) stoichiometry.

quiz.q:
Misrun or cold shut?

Problem. A thin fin on a casting is simply absent — no metal reached that section at all. A separate casting shows a fused-looking seam where two streams met, but the seam pulls apart easily under light stress, with an oxide film visible inside. Identify each defect and state one distinct corrective action for each.
quiz.solution: Solution. The absent fin is a misrun — the stream froze before reaching that section; corrective actions include more superheat or faster filling. The weak, oxide-lined seam is a cold shut — two streams met too cold to truly fuse; correction additionally requires gating that avoids opposed, long-path streams meeting head-on, not superheat alone.

quiz.q: The patternmaker's shrinkage allowance compensates for:
choices: Solid contraction, after the casting is fully frozen
quiz.solution: Only solid-state contraction is left for the pattern oversize to correct.

quiz.q: Low sand permeability combined with high moisture is a classic setup for:
quiz.solution: Steam that cannot vent through the sand is forced into the metal instead.

quiz.q: Foundry fluidity is:
choices: A measured quantity — the distance a test channel fills before freezing shut
quiz.solution: Fluidity is an empirical race between flow and mold-controlled freezing, not a viscosity value.

quiz.q: A misrun differs from a cold shut in that:
choices: A misrun is a section the metal never reached; a cold shut is two streams that met too cold to fuse
They are the same defect with two names
A misrun only occurs in permanent molds
A cold shut is always a gas defect
quiz.solution: Different causes mean different fixes, even though both stem from lost fluidity.

quiz.q: Sievert's law, [H] = S₀√(p/p₀), has a square root because:
choices: Hydrogen gas dissolves by splitting into two atoms, H₂ ⇌ 2[H]
It is an empirical curve fit with no physical basis
Pressure always relates to concentration by a square root
It only applies at very high temperature
quiz.solution: The exponent comes directly from the reaction's stoichiometry.

quiz.q: Pinhole porosity forms because:
choices: Gas solubility drops sharply as the metal solidifies, rejecting dissolved gas
The mold sand is too permeable
quiz.solution: The liquid-to-solid solubility cliff is the entire gas-porosity mechanism.

quiz.q: Round, smooth-walled pores versus jagged, angular voids distinguish:
quiz.solution: Morphology is the diagnostic signature separating these two defect families.

quiz.q: The correct working method for diagnosing a casting defect is:
choices: Read the morphology, trace it to its physical cause, then let the cause pick the fix
Always assume gas porosity by default
Apply the same fix regardless of appearance
quiz.solution: Appearance alone can mislead — cause, not appearance, should select the corrective action.

3.kuwait:
A foundry's molding-sand test sheet — green compressive strength, permeability, moisture, logged shift by shift — predicts that shop's defect profile before a single casting is sectioned: low permeability paired with high moisture reads as a blowhole risk; low strength reads as a penetration-and-wash risk; over-rammed strong sand with hot metal reads as a hot-tear risk. A metallurgist reading that sheet is running §1 in reverse, from cause toward prediction rather than from defect toward diagnosis.

Sectioning a rejected casting and reading its pore morphology under a loupe — round and smooth versus jagged and angular — is standard first-pass failure triage in any melt shop, cheaper and faster than radiography for an initial call on whether a batch problem is gas-related (atmosphere, moisture, melt practice) or feeding-related (riser or gating design), before deciding which department's process actually needs adjusting.
